channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Field Demonstration of Trusted-Node QKD over Deployed Single-Mode and Multi-Core Fiber Infrastructure
item.description:
Curator's Take: This field demonstration represents a crucial step toward making quantum key distribution commercially viable by proving it can operate within real-world telecommunications infrastructure rather than controlled laboratory conditions. The collaboration between multiple Swedish universities showcases how trusted-node QKD can be integrated into existing fiber networks, potentially paving the way for quantum-secured communications at scale. What makes this particularly significant is the use of both single-mode and multi-core fiber infrastructure, demonstrating the flexibility needed for practical deployment across diverse network architectures. This work bridges the gap between quantum cryptography research and the telecommunications industry's need for quantum-safe security solutions in an era of advancing quantum computing threats.

A collaborative research team representing Linköping University, KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Stockholm University, and Chalmers University of Technology has reported the physical deployment of a long-haul, trusted-node Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) link integrated into a dynamically reconfigurable telecommunications network. Detailed in an open-access preprint on arXiv, the field trial established secure quantum channels over [...] The post Field Demonstration of Trusted-Node QKD over Deployed Single-Mode and Multi-Core Fiber Infrastructure appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Cloud-tested quantum noise model predicts superconducting qubit errors with sevenfold better accuracy
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in quantum error prediction represents a crucial step toward more reliable quantum computing, as accurately modeling and predicting errors is essential for developing effective error correction schemes. The sevenfold improvement in predictive accuracy achieved by the Johns Hopkins team could significantly accelerate the development of fault-tolerant quantum systems by giving researchers much better insight into when and why qubits fail. What makes this work particularly valuable is its practical, cloud-tested approach using real superconducting processors rather than theoretical models, meaning the framework can be immediately applied to current quantum hardware. This enhanced error prediction capability brings us closer to the holy grail of quantum computing: systems that can reliably perform calculations longer than their individual components naturally allow.

Researchers from the Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory (APL) in Laurel, Maryland, and Johns Hopkins University in Baltimore have developed a practical, comprehensive noise-modeling framework for a popular class of superconducting quantum processors. Their work, published in the journal PRX Quantum, offers a sevenfold improvement in predictive accuracy over existing approaches.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Physicists create new family of Schrödinger-cat states
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough expands the quantum toolkit by developing new types of superposition states that could enhance the sensitivity of quantum sensors used in gravitational wave detection, magnetic field measurements, and atomic clocks. While Schrödinger cat states have been created before, this new "family" of states suggests researchers have found ways to engineer more robust or specialized quantum superpositions that maintain their delicate quantum properties longer or perform better in specific applications. The work represents important progress in quantum state engineering, which is fundamental not just for quantum computing but for the emerging field of quantum sensing that promises to revolutionize precision measurements in physics and beyond. These advances in creating and controlling quantum superpositions could lead to sensors capable of detecting phenomena currently beyond our measurement capabilities.

Quantum mechanics, unlike classical physics, allows objects to exist in more than one state at the same time. This idea is often illustrated by Schrödinger's cat, imagined as being both alive and dead until it is observed. In the laboratory, physicists can create less dramatic but very real versions of this effect by placing atoms, light or motion into two distinct quantum states at once. Creating and controlling these superpositions is essential for applications ranging from quantum computing to precision timekeeping.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: On the viability of Transatlantic Quantum Entanglement Distribution using Combined Satellite and Stratospheric Relay Nodes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum networking's biggest challenges: how to practically distribute entangled photons across ocean-spanning distances without quantum repeaters, which remain technologically elusive. The proposed hybrid architecture cleverly combines low Earth orbit satellites with stratospheric platforms to achieve entanglement rates nearly 100 times higher than traditional satellite-only approaches, potentially making intercontinental quantum communication viable with today's technology. What makes this particularly exciting is that the system avoids the need for quantum memories or repeaters while still delivering millions of secure key bits annually across the Atlantic, bringing us significantly closer to a practical global quantum internet. The use of maneuverable stratospheric platforms also offers a compelling solution to weather disruptions that have plagued ground-based quantum communication links.

To explore the pathways toward establishing a global quantum network, we investigate several link architectures for transatlantic quantum entanglement distribution over a 6,500 km ground distance. We define free-space link configurations involving satellites and stratospheric high altitude platforms (HAPs), using today's technology and without relying on quantum memories and repeaters. Considering link budgets, space radiation, orbital characteristics, and system complexity we find that a hybrid architecture consisting of an entangled photon source located on a low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite supported by two passive optical relays located on HAPs provides the overall highest entanglement distribution rate. In addition, the satellite HAP architecture offers practical advantages in payload design and launch requirements, and the ability to lower the weather-related link interruptions assuming some maneuverability of HAPs. Overall, this hybrid configuration yields on the order of 5X10^6 secure key bits per year using 30 cm aperture ground receivers, nearly two orders of magnitude higher than achievable with a single MEO satellite and 1 m aperture ground receivers. Our results highlight the major benefits of hybrid satellite HAP architectures by reducing system complexity while enabling scalable and more accessible long-range quantum communication networks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: From Pauli Strings to Quantum Dynamics: A Unified Characterization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work provides a powerful mathematical framework that could significantly streamline how quantum algorithm designers analyze and optimize their circuits. By establishing deep connections between Pauli strings and Clifford group structures, the researchers have created efficient algorithms for determining what quantum states and operations are reachable with given sets of quantum gates - a fundamental question that's typically computationally intensive to answer. The framework's applications span from variational quantum algorithms (like QAOA) to many-body physics simulations, offering practical tools for understanding when quantum circuits can efficiently explore desired quantum state spaces. Perhaps most importantly, the invariant-based approach gives researchers a systematic way to identify structure in quantum systems that can be exploited for computational advantage, potentially leading to more efficient quantum algorithm designs.

Understanding the dynamical properties of quantum systems is an essential task in quantum computing, quantum control, and many-body physics. Tools such as representation theory and Lie theory provide crucial information on reachability and computational power. However, this information can be difficult to access exactly or compute efficiently for arbitrary generating sets. Here we focus on the setting of Pauli strings, which satisfy numerous exceptional properties that simplify the problem. We find deep connections between Pauli Lie algebras and certain subgroups of the Clifford group generated by transvections, through the symplectic properties of the Pauli strings. This allows us to give an invariant-based perspective on these objects and their reachability, in the language of Pauli orbits, symmetries, and invariant subspaces. The invariant-based approach provides efficient algorithms for identifying Lie algebras and orbits, as well as a simple framework for analyzing structured Pauli generating sets. We also show in an elementary way that Clifford subgroups generated by transvections provide 3-designs for the corresponding Pauli Lie groups. We illustrate the framework through structured examples from variational quantum algorithms, restricted quantum computation, many-body systems, and random circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Adaptive directional gradients for parameterised quantum circuits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most practical bottlenecks in quantum machine learning: the prohibitive measurement cost of training large parameterized quantum circuits, where traditional gradient estimation methods require shots that scale linearly with the number of parameters. The proposed forward gradient framework cleverly adapts automatic differentiation techniques from classical machine learning to the quantum realm, offering a tunable approach that can dramatically reduce measurement overhead while maintaining mathematical rigor. The demonstration of training quantum neural networks with nearly 1800 parameters represents a significant scaling achievement, potentially making quantum machine learning more feasible on current noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices. This work bridges theoretical quantum computing with practical optimization concerns, offering researchers a new toolkit for making quantum algorithms more resource-efficient in real-world applications.

Training parameterised quantum circuits (PQCs) on quantum hardware is bottlenecked by the measurement cost of gradient estimation, which under the parameter-shift rule scales linearly in the number of trainable parameters and dominates the total shot budget of training at scale. In this work, we propose a framework of forward gradient estimators for PQCs, based on the forward mode of automatic differentiation, that yields an unbiased estimator of the gradient by averaging a freely tunable number of random directional derivatives and recovers SPSA, random coordinate descent, and the parameter-shift rule as limiting cases, with no ancilla qubits or controlled-gate overhead. We prove that stochastic quantum forward gradient descent converges under standard assumptions, with an explicit second-moment expansion that interpolates between the single-direction extreme of SPSA and the full-gradient extreme of parameter-shift. Within this framework we derive QUIVER (Quantum Iterative V-adaptive Estimator Rule), an adaptive optimiser for parameterised circuits whose update rule follows from a closed-form minimum measurement-cost allocation. We show numerically that forward gradients train Hamming-weight-preserving orthogonal quantum neural networks with up to 60 qubits and 1770 parameters on the ECG5000 and MNIST datasets orders of magnitude more efficiently than the parameter-shift rule. We also demonstrate that our proposed QUIVER optimiser can outperform iCANS and gCANS measurement-frugal optimisers on optimisation problems using the quantum approximate optimisation algorithm and quantum simulation with the variational quantum eigensolver.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A Bell-State Extension of Loop-Back Quantum Key Distribution
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a clever evolution of quantum key distribution that addresses fundamental vulnerabilities in bidirectional protocols by leveraging Bell states as hidden references. The breakthrough lies in how Alice's private knowledge of the initial entangled state creates an asymmetric advantage that makes eavesdropping attempts significantly more detectable, boosting intrusion detection to roughly 75% per communication round. What makes this particularly compelling is that it maintains the practical benefits of Loop-Back QKD's simplified remote terminals while dramatically expanding the usable signal space beyond the restrictive subspaces that have limited previous two-way quantum cryptography schemes. This work represents meaningful progress toward more robust and efficient quantum communication networks that could better resist sophisticated attacks while remaining implementable with current technology.

Bidirectional quantum key distribution (QKD) protocols face persistent challenges related to classical disclosure, confinement of the signal space to predictable subspaces, and limited detectability under substitution or entanglement-swapping attacks. In this work, we present a Bell-state extension of the Loop-Back QKD architecture that improves efficiency and detectability while preserving its defining feature of a simplified, measurement-free remote terminal. The protocol employs entangled Bell states together with deterministic local Pauli encoding at the remote node. A central element is that Alice privately prepares and knows the initial Bell state, which serves as a hidden reference enabling her to interpret the Bell-state transition induced by Bob, while preventing an adversary from reconstructing the encoding without access to this reference. By exploiting both intra- and inter-family Bell transitions, the scheme expands the effective signal space beyond the subspace restrictions of earlier two-way protocols. Alice performs a Bell-state measurement to deterministically infer Bob's operation without any basis sifting. Although the traveling subsystem remains locally maximally mixed, concealing the initial Bell family amplifies disturbance under separable substitution strategies, yielding an intrinsic detection probability of approximately 3/4 per round. From an efficiency perspective, the protocol lifts the intrinsic post-selection limitation of single-qubit Loop-Back schemes: the effective throughput is bounded only by the Bell-state measurement success probability, reaching up to 50% in linear-optical implementations. These features make the proposed scheme particularly suitable for mobile or edge-based QKD scenarios, where passive remote nodes must operate under high loss and limited interaction times.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy of a semiconductor charge qubit coupled to a high-impedance resonator
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research provides crucial insights into the fundamental noise mechanisms that limit quantum coherence in hybrid semiconductor-superconductor systems, which are emerging as promising platforms for quantum computing due to their electrical tunability and potential for scalability. The frequency-resolved spectroscopy approach is particularly valuable because it allows researchers to systematically identify and separate different decoherence sources across a broad energy range, revealing that phonon interactions dominate at higher frequencies while charge noise prevails at lower frequencies. The cubic frequency scaling they observed for relaxation rates offers direct experimental confirmation of theoretical predictions about how charge qubits interact with their phonon environment, providing a roadmap for engineering better hybrid devices. This type of detailed decoherence mapping will be essential for optimizing future quantum processors that combine the best features of semiconductor and superconducting technologies.

Superconducting resonators coupled to semiconductor quantum dots provide a powerful platform to investigate light-matter interaction and decoherence mechanisms in solid-state quantum systems. Here we study a hybrid circuit quantum electrodynamics architecture consisting of a GaAs double-quantum-dot charge qubit capacitively coupled to a high-impedance, frequency-tunable SQUID-array resonator. By tuning the qubit transition frequency over the range $ω_\mathrm{q}/2π\sim 3$-$6$ GHz, we perform frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy of the charge qubit across a broad energy window. Time-resolved measurements enable us to disentangle relaxation and pure dephasing processes and to identify distinct decoherence regimes as a function of qubit frequency. We find that at lower frequencies ($\leq 4.5$ GHz) dephasing dominates the qubit linewidth, whereas at higher frequencies energy relaxation becomes the leading contribution. The measured frequency dependence of the relaxation rate exhibits a cubic scaling, consistent with charge-qubit decay dominated by coupling to a piezoelectric phonon bath and providing frequency-resolved access to the corresponding phonon-induced spectral density. Our results show that hybrid semiconductor--superconducting circuits can serve as sensitive spectroscopic tools to probe microscopic decoherence mechanisms relevant for a wide range of hybrid quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: WiMi Explores Quantum Algorithms for Multi-Dimensional Data Pooling
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights WiMi's development of quantum algorithms that combine the Quantum Haar Transform with partial measurement techniques to optimize multi-dimensional data pooling, potentially offering significant advantages for processing complex datasets in AR and holographic applications. The approach represents an intriguing intersection of quantum computing with practical data processing challenges, where quantum algorithms could provide exponential speedups for the massive data manipulation required in real-time holographic rendering. While WiMi is primarily known as an AR company rather than a quantum computing specialist, their exploration of variational quantum algorithms for data pooling demonstrates how quantum techniques are beginning to permeate diverse industries beyond traditional quantum research labs. The practical impact will largely depend on whether these quantum pooling methods can be implemented on near-term quantum devices and deliver measurable performance gains over classical alternatives.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — WiMi Hologram Cloud Inc. (NASDAQ: WiMi) (“WiMi” or the “Company”), a leading global Hologram Augmented Reality (“AR”) Technology provider, is exploring multi-dimensional pooling optimization technology under the variational quantum algorithm framework, proposing an innovative solution that integrates the Quantum Haar Transform (QHT) with quantum partial measurement, and constructing a quantum pooling […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement Generation through Coherent and Non-Coherent Control
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: how to reliably create entangled states, the quantum correlations that power everything from quantum algorithms to quantum communication protocols. The authors demonstrate that entanglement can be generated deterministically by coherently superposing different sequences of local operations, offering a new pathway beyond traditional approaches that often rely on specific two-qubit gates or measurements. What makes this particularly intriguing is their extension to noisy, realistic scenarios where they show that useful entanglement can still emerge even when operations are implemented stochastically through quantum channels with indefinite causal order. This work could inform the design of more robust entanglement generation protocols for near-term quantum devices, where noise and imperfect control are unavoidable realities.

The controlled generation of quantum entanglement from separable states remains a central challenge in quantum information science. Here, we investigate entanglement generation using two related control paradigms: coherent path superposition of local unitary operations and stochastic implementations of Pauli channels under coherent control. We show that entangled states belonging to the Bell, GHZ and W classes, can be deterministically generated from fully separable inputs by coherently superposing alternative sets of local unitary transformations. Conditions on the local operators for entanglement generation are derived, and the resulting states are shown to be locally unitary equivalent to standard multipartite entangled states. We further extend the analysis to noisy scenarios, where separable mixed states evolve through pairs of Pauli channels arranged in path-superposition and indefinite causal order configurations. Closed-form expressions for the output states are obtained, and entanglement is quantified using concurrence. By exploring representative channel families across their parameter space, we identify regimes where stochastic entanglement emerges, determine the associated success probabilities, and characterize trade-offs between entanglement and purity.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Probabilistically Checking Quantum Proofs, with Interaction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough provides the first quantum analogue of interactive oracle proofs, creating a new framework for efficiently verifying quantum computations by reading only a tiny fraction of the quantum proof data. The work is particularly significant because it offers the first information-theoretically sound method for locally checking quantum proofs in the absence of a quantum PCP theorem, which has remained one of the most elusive problems in quantum complexity theory. By combining quantum locally testable codes with classical probabilistic verification techniques, the research opens new pathways for developing practical quantum verification protocols that could be crucial as quantum computers scale up. This represents a fundamental advance in quantum complexity theory that could eventually lead to more efficient ways to verify the correctness of quantum algorithms and simulations.

The model of interactive oracle proofs (IOP) generalizes the notion of probabilistically checkable proof (PCP), in which a static proof is verified probabilistically by querying a small number of bits, to the interactive setting: a polynomial-time verifier interacts with an unbounded prover, but is restricted to only reading a small number of bits, in total, from the messages sent by the prover. IOPs provide a relaxed setting in which to study local probabilistic verification. They have proved instrumental in devising efficient methods for verification through subsequent compilation into non-interactive or succinct protocols. We study a quantum analogue of interactive oracle proofs (qIOP) in which the verifier and communication are both allowed to be quantum; yet the verifier is restricted to perform measurements only on a small number of qubits received from the prover. Our main result is a qIOP for any language in QMA, in which the total communication is polynomial but the verifier only reads a polylogarithmic number of qubits in total. The protocol has completeness parameter exponentially close to $1$ and soundness bounded away from $1$ by a constant. In the absence of a quantum PCP theorem, this provides the first information-theoretically sound local and robust characterization of QMA, albeit interactive. Our protocol combines the use of a quantum locally testable code (LTC) with classical techniques, notably probabilistically checkable proofs of proximity (PCPP). We avoid the necessity for complex multi-qubit tests employed in other settings by leveraging the local indistinguishability property of the quantum LTC.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Algebraic Kolmogorov--Arnold representation theorem for quantum measurement
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a fascinating bridge between classical mathematical theory and quantum sensing applications by adapting the famous Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem for quantum measurements. The key breakthrough is showing that any physical property of multi-qubit states can be decomposed using just local measurements and simple polynomial operations, potentially simplifying the design of quantum sensors and measurement protocols. What makes this particularly exciting is the proven stability against noise and adversarial attacks - a major advantage over classical versions of the theorem that are notoriously fragile. This mathematical foundation could lead to more robust and efficient quantum sensing architectures, especially valuable as quantum devices scale up and face increasing environmental perturbations.

We establish an operational framework connecting the classical Kolmogorov--Arnold (KA) representation theorem to quantum information theory. By introducing and proving an algebraic, bounded-degree polynomial version of the theorem, we demonstrate that any target physical property of an unentangled multi-qubit product state can be exactly decomposed using a finite, fixed set of local <<inner>> observables and a shallow architecture of univariate polynomials. We further analyze the stability of this Quantum Kolmogorov--Arnold (QKA) representation under adversarial perturbations. In stark contrast to the pathological instabilities and severe reparameterization sensitivities inherent to the classical Kolmogorov--Arnold representation theorem, our algebraic quantum framework exhibits remarkable resilience. We prove that the representation remains stable against bounded physical perturbations acting on the inner measurement operators, and show via the Heisenberg picture that it is inherently immune to adversarial quantum channel attacks acting on the input states.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Agentic Operating System for Quantum R&D Teams to Debut During Live Event
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a fascinating intersection between AI agents and quantum research operations, suggesting that administrative and workflow bottlenecks may be significantly hampering quantum R&D progress. The concept of an "agentic operating system" specifically designed for quantum teams addresses a real pain point in the field, where complex experimental setups, resource coordination, and interdisciplinary collaboration often create operational friction that slows scientific advancement. While the technical details remain to be seen, the focus on optimizing research workflows could prove as impactful as hardware improvements themselves, especially as quantum teams scale up and tackle increasingly complex problems. This represents a growing recognition that quantum computing's success depends not just on better qubits, but on better ways of organizing and accelerating the research process itself.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Are operating inefficiencies holding quantum research and development back? It’s an important question — and one that’s being addressed by The Quantum Insider during an upcoming live panel discussion, “Are Agents the Secret to Quantum R&D Success?” on Tuesday, July 7. During this webinar, hosted by Haiqu, industry experts from […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Hardware-Aware QAOA for Honeypot Traffic Partitioning on 100+ Qubit IBM Quantum Processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant milestone in demonstrating quantum algorithms on real-world cybersecurity problems, scaling QAOA implementations up to 110 qubits across multiple IBM quantum processors to tackle network traffic analysis for DDoS mitigation. While the authors acknowledge that classical methods currently outperform their quantum approach, the work provides crucial benchmarking infrastructure for evaluating how different quantum hardware architectures handle practical optimization problems. The focus on "utility scale" demonstrates that quantum algorithms can now process realistic problem sizes, even if quantum advantage remains elusive, and the systematic comparison of routing overhead across different IBM backends offers valuable insights for near-term quantum application development. This kind of hardware-aware benchmarking is essential for understanding when and where quantum computers might eventually surpass classical methods in cybersecurity applications.

Denial-of-service (DoS) and distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) mitigation requires separating malicious traffic from benign traffic while minimizing disruption to legitimate users. Prior work proposed mapping honeypot traffic partitioning to a weighted MaxCut problem and solving the resulting graphs with variational quantum algorithms. We extend this proof of principle direction with a reproducible event-level honeypot-to-QUBO pipeline, labeled temporal bipartite benchmark graphs with 16, 32, 66, and 110 event nodes, QAOA executions on IBM quantum hardware, classical heuristic baselines, a noiseless matrix product state reference, and a routing overhead analysis across quantum processor architectures. The largest benchmark is a 110-node, 181-edge instance executed on three IBM backends. Our results show that a shallow QAOA can execute real traffic partitioning workloads at the utility scale, while backend architecture and routing overhead affect objective quality, security metrics, and observed runtime. Because simple classical heuristics can solve the current labeled benchmark graphs, these experiments are not a quantum advantage claim. Instead, we deliberately use a fixed, shallow QAOA implementation to enable controlled comparisons across problem sizes and hardware architectures. This work establishes a hardware feasibility and architecture benchmark framework, and demonstrates that MaxCut cost, security quality, routing overhead, and runtime must be reported as separate metrics for cybersecurity relevant quantum optimization.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Researchers Craft a New, Simple Recipe for Highly Entangled Quantum States
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough from University of Chicago researchers addresses one of quantum computing's fundamental challenges: creating highly entangled quantum states with simpler, more practical methods. Traditional approaches for generating complex entangled states require intricate experimental setups with numerous precisely controlled components, making them difficult to scale and prone to errors. The development of a simplified "recipe" for producing these essential quantum states could significantly lower the barriers for building quantum sensors, computers, and other quantum technologies. This work represents the kind of foundational advance that could accelerate the transition from laboratory demonstrations to practical quantum devices by making entanglement generation more accessible and reliable.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Building useful quantum technologies—from sensors to computers—requires generating highly complex entangled states, in which the properties of particles are deeply intertwined. Producing such states has traditionally required complex tools and carefully engineered setups with many parts. Now, researchers at the University of Chicago Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering (UChicago PME) […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement-assisted continuous-variable concatenated codes for encoding qubits or oscillators
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant advance in quantum error correction by cleverly combining two powerful concepts: entanglement-assisted codes that leverage pre-shared quantum entanglement to boost error correction rates, and concatenated coding schemes that chain different types of codes together for enhanced performance. The work bridges discrete qubit systems with continuous-variable quantum systems using Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill codes, creating hybrid architectures that could be particularly valuable for photonic quantum computers and trapped-ion systems operating in continuous regimes. What makes this especially promising is the demonstrated ability to suppress both position and momentum errors simultaneously while using fewer physical resources than traditional approaches, potentially offering a more efficient path to fault-tolerant quantum computing. The mathematical framework developed here opens new possibilities for tailoring error correction strategies to the specific noise characteristics of different quantum hardware platforms.

Entanglement-assisted (EA) stabilizer codes enhance the rate of error correction in relation to codes with no pre-shared entanglement. Meanwhile, bosonic error-correcting codes, such as the Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) code, can be concatenated with qubit stabilizer codes to significantly reduce the logical failure probability of those stabilizer codes. First, we combine the above two concepts to propose an EA version of the qubit-into-oscillators concatenated code that chains an EA-stabilizer (outer) code with a GKP (inner) code. As an example we present a three-qubit EA-repetition concatenated with a GKP code. Second, we propose an EA version of the non-Gaussian oscillator-into-oscillators concatenated code that chains a GKP (outer) code with an EA-stabilizer (inner) code. As an example we present a GKP code concatenated with a three-qubit EA repetition code that uses two maximally entangled modes (emodes) and suppresses the variances of both position and momentum quadrature errors of a data mode. Furthermore, we generalize the latter example to a family of GKP code concatenated with a $n$-qubit EA repetition code that uses ${n-1}$ emodes and suppresses the variances of both position and momentum quadrature errors of a data mode by a factor ${1/n}$.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: How to Benchmark Logical Qubit Claims
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a crucial shift happening in quantum computing evaluation as the field matures beyond simple qubit counting. As companies race to demonstrate fault-tolerant quantum computers, the focus is rightfully moving from raw physical qubits to logical qubits that can actually perform error-corrected computation reliably. The challenge lies in establishing standardized benchmarks for logical qubit performance, since different approaches to quantum error correction make direct comparisons between systems increasingly complex. This shift in measurement philosophy reflects the quantum industry's transition from proof-of-concept demonstrations to building machines that can solve real-world problems.

Insider Brief As the quantum computing sector has evolved, so has the way performance is measured. Early on, qubit count — the number of quantum bits in a processor — was viewed as a key benchmark. Today, researchers increasingly argue that raw qubit count matters less than the number of logical qubits, which can reliably […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Range-controlled entanglement in Lindbladian skin states of monitored fermions
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article reveals a fascinating connection between quantum dissipation and entanglement that could reshape how we think about controlling quantum states. The researchers show that by carefully tuning how far quantum particles can "hop" in a dissipative system, they can dial the entanglement from simple area-law scaling to more complex sub-volume-law behavior—essentially discovering a new knob for quantum state engineering. This work bridges two major areas of quantum physics: non-Hermitian quantum mechanics (where energy isn't conserved) and quantum many-body systems, potentially opening new pathways for creating exotic quantum states that are impossible with standard unitary evolution. The ability to precisely control entanglement through dissipation could prove valuable for quantum error correction schemes and novel quantum algorithms that harness non-equilibrium dynamics.

Reservoir engineering can stabilize states inaccessible to unitary dynamics. Directed particle-conserving dissipation creates Lindbladian skin states, where Pauli exclusion turns edge accumulation into a many-body density imbalance. In a monitored fermion chain with tunable hopping range, we identify, within a Gaussian trajectory approximation, two finite-size scaling regimes: short-range hopping is consistent with complete skin accumulation and area-law entanglement, whereas sufficiently long-range hopping produces a finite bulk tail and effective algebraic sub-volume-law entanglement. Dissipation and coherent hopping thus jointly control skin localization and quantum entanglement, highlighting their close interconnection.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Satellite-Based Quantum Communication: Performance Evaluation of Discrete-Variable Quantum Key Distribution Protocols
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive thesis tackles one of quantum cryptography's biggest practical challenges: making quantum key distribution work reliably across the vast distances that only satellites can bridge. While ground-based quantum networks are limited to a few hundred kilometers due to fiber losses, satellite-based QKD promises truly global quantum-secure communications by beaming quantum states through space. The research's detailed modeling of atmospheric effects like turbulence and pointing errors addresses real-world engineering hurdles that have plagued early satellite QKD demonstrations, potentially accelerating the timeline for operational quantum communication networks. This work becomes increasingly critical as organizations worldwide race to deploy quantum-safe communications before large-scale quantum computers arrive to break today's encryption standards.

Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) has emerged as a fundamentally secure approach to communication in the era of quantum computing, offering protection against threats posed to classical cryptographic schemes such as RSA and Diffie-Hellman. This thesis presents a comprehensive performance analysis of satellite-based QKD protocols, focusing on both prepare-and-measure and entanglement-based schemes under realistic atmospheric and operational conditions. The study begins by introducing the theoretical foundations of quantum communication, including qubits, entanglement, and quantum entropy, and motivates the need for satellite-based QKD to overcome the distance limitations of fiber-based systems. Subsequently, the thesis evaluates four prominent QKD protocols-BB84, B92, BBM92, and E91-using a circular beam propagation model that incorporates atmospheric effects such as diffraction, turbulence, attenuation, and pointing errors, along with environmental noise contributions for uplink and downlink. Comparative numerical simulations reveal that protocol performance is strongly influenced by channel asymmetries, beam propagation characteristics, and noise, providing guidance on optimal protocol selection for low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite links. The research further investigates high-dimensional (HD) QKD protocols, specifically HD-BB84 and HD-Extended B92, using the elliptic-beam approximation to account for turbulence-induced distortions for both uplink and downlink. Simulations under vary ing system dimensions, weather conditions, and zenith angles demonstrate that HD-BB84 achieves higher key rates, superior noise tolerance, and more favorable probability distributions of the key rate compared to HD-Extended B92, highlighting the advantages of high-dimensional encoding for robust satellite-based QKD.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Transformation-Response Framework: An Operational Reformulation of Quantum Mechanics
item.description:
Curator's Take: This arXiv paper presents a bold attempt to rebuild quantum mechanics from the ground up using only operational measurements and group theory, potentially offering the most minimalist foundation for quantum theory to date. The transformation-response framework is particularly intriguing because it derives all the familiar quantum formalism—including the Schrödinger equation and Feynman path integrals—from a single postulate about positive-definite response functions, while remaining agnostic about the underlying nature of time and space. What makes this approach especially compelling is its introduction of "product order positivity," a new mathematical constraint that could lead to experimentally testable predictions, giving physicists a way to probe whether this reformulation captures something deeper about quantum reality. While highly theoretical, this kind of foundational work has historically led to practical breakthroughs—quantum computing itself emerged from similar deep questions about the mathematical structure of quantum mechanics.

We present the transformation-response framework, an operational reformulation of quantum mechanics. A quantum state is not a Hilbert space object but the catalog of a system's responses to all physical transformations: for each operation $g$ from the system's local group $G$, an interference experiment gives a complex value $χ(g)$. The collection $\{χ(g): g\in G \}$ is the characteristic function and defines the state. The only postulate is that $χ$ is positive-definite, encoding the requirement that no superposition of transformations yields negative probability. From this single assumption, the entire standard formalism is derived: Hilbert space via GNS construction, Born rule via Bochner theorem, Schrödinger equation from group automorphisms, and especially Feynman path integral as a Trotter limit. The framework is background-independent and time-neutral: time is a coordinate along a one-parameter subgroup of $G$. It also reveals a new physical constraint, product order positivity, which may lead to testable predictions. The framework provides a unified, economical, and falsifiable foundation for quantum theory rooted in operational primitives.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Mechanical Studies of Photodissociation Dynamics on Quantum Computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a significant step toward using quantum computers for real-world chemistry problems, specifically demonstrating how quantum algorithms can simulate molecular photodissociation dynamics - the process where molecules break apart when hit by light. The researchers successfully implemented their algorithm on the NOCl molecule, showing that quantum computers can handle the complex quantum mechanical calculations needed to understand how chemical bonds break, which classical computers struggle with for larger systems. What's particularly encouraging is their demonstration that the algorithm remains robust even with the noise and errors present in today's quantum devices, suggesting this approach could be practical on near-term quantum computers. This work opens the door for quantum computers to eventually tackle larger, more complex molecular systems that are completely beyond classical simulation capabilities, potentially revolutionizing computational chemistry and our understanding of photochemical processes.

Theoretical quantum dynamics calculations scale deeply with system size, rendering classical calculations intractable for complex systems. While quantum computing offers a natural solution, its application to nuclear quantum dynamics remains scarce. Here, we present a quantum algorithm to study photodissociation dynamics on quantum computers, benchmarked on the NOCl molecule. The wavefunction is propagated via a split-operator method, utilizing the Quantum Fourier Transform and unitary transformation matrix to switch representations. To impose outgoing boundary conditions on a truncated grid, we use a non-unitary absorbing potential propagator, implemented through a dilation scheme. The photodissociation cross section is calculated from the auto-correlation function, which is extracted using the Hadamard test. Our quantum computing results agree well with benchmarks under ideal conditions, and we further demonstrate that the algorithm is robust to noise and statistical sampling errors, indicating the promising application of noisy devices to quantum dynamics studies.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Impact of gate-voltage noise on silicon spin-qubit variational quantum eigensolvers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical challenge for silicon spin qubits by developing the first comprehensive simulation framework that connects real-world voltage noise all the way through to algorithmic performance in quantum chemistry applications. The findings reveal that two-qubit exchange gates are about ten times more vulnerable to charge noise than single-qubit operations, providing crucial guidance for hardware designers working to scale up silicon quantum processors. By using the Variational Quantum Eigensolver as a testbed, the authors bridge the gap between low-level hardware imperfections and high-level algorithm accuracy, offering a roadmap for co-optimizing silicon qubit control systems and quantum algorithms. This work is particularly timely as silicon spin qubits emerge as strong contenders for large-scale quantum computers, since understanding and mitigating charge noise will be essential for achieving quantum advantage in practical applications.

Quantum computers offer a route to outperform classical methods in tasks such as molecular simulation, motivating hybrid algorithms like the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) for near-term devices. Silicon spin qubits are a promising platform for scalable quantum computation, but their performance is limited by hardware imperfections -- most notably charge-noise-induced potential fluctuations and static miscalibration of gate-electrode voltages -- which degrade quantum gate fidelities and, ultimately, algorithmic accuracy. Here we develop a hardware-algorithm co-simulation framework for silicon quantum-dot processors that links 3D electrostatics to effective $g$-factors and exchange couplings, and propagates voltage-level noise through realistic control pulses. Using VQE for $\mathrm{H}_2$ ground-state energy estimation as a circuit-level testbed, we study both static scaling/offset errors on the gate-electrode voltages and stochastic fluctuations modeled as random-telegraph noise with tunable amplitudes and switching times. At the gate level, we show that exchange-based two-qubit gates are roughly an order of magnitude more sensitive to these types of noise than ESR-driven single-qubit rotations. Quantum process tomography and Kraus-operator analysis further distinguish coherent and incoherent contributions and quantify the fraction of error that is, in principle, correctable by a compensating unitary. Embedding these noise models into the VQE circuit, we identify regimes of miscalibration strength and noise switching time compatible with chemically accurate energy estimates, and discuss how statistical post-processing based on the full distribution of noisy energy estimates could further improve accuracy.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Graph Neural Networks for Fast Operator Selection in Adaptive VQE
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most computationally expensive bottlenecks in adaptive variational quantum algorithms - the need to evaluate gradients across entire operator pools at each iteration, which becomes prohibitively costly for complex quantum systems. By reformulating operator selection as a graph learning problem, the authors developed a neural network that can predict which quantum operators to add next based on the system's interaction structure and current quantum state, dramatically reducing the classical overhead that often dominates these hybrid algorithms. The approach shows promising transferability from spin systems to molecular problems, suggesting it could accelerate quantum chemistry simulations where ADAPT-VQE has shown great promise but suffered from scalability issues. This work represents a compelling fusion of classical machine learning and quantum algorithm design that could make adaptive quantum algorithms practical for larger, more realistic quantum systems.

Adaptive variational quantum algorithms like ADAPT-VQE construct tailored ansätze by iteratively selecting operators from a pool using gradient-based criteria. While this avoids oversized parameter spaces, repeatedly scanning the full pool incurs a classical cost that scales linearly with pool size-a major bottleneck for systems with long-range interactions or large operator sets. Here, we reformulate adaptive operator selection as a graph-based decision problem and introduce a graph neural network (GNN) policy that predicts the next entangling operator directly from the interaction graph and state-dependent observables. Training data are generated from exact simulations of disordered long-range spin chains, using gradient magnitudes as supervision signals. The learned policy accurately reproduces the dominant structure of the greedy gradient-based selection rule, significantly outperforming heuristics based solely on interaction strength. Integrated into a variational quantum eigensolver (VQE) workflow, this GNN-VQE approach achieves energy errors close to standard ADAPT-VQE while drastically reducing full-pool gradient evaluations. To test transferability beyond spin models, we evaluate the policy on small active-space molecular benchmarks (LiH and BeH_$2$). We find the GNN is highly effective as a shortlist generator: exact rescoring over just a few GNN-proposed candidates recovers near-oracle rollout behavior while searching only a small fraction of the pool. These results demonstrate that adaptive circuit construction contains learnable structure that can be exploited to accelerate variational quantum algorithms.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Randomized simulation of quantum channels using small ancilla
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates a fundamental quantum computing principle: you can efficiently simulate complex quantum operations using surprisingly few extra qubits, as long as you're willing to accept some probability of failure. The researchers proved that any unital quantum channel (operations that preserve certain quantum properties) can be simulated with only logarithmic overhead in ancilla qubits while maintaining constant success probability—a massive improvement over naive approaches that might require exponential resources. This work has profound implications for quantum algorithm design and error correction, potentially enabling more efficient implementations of quantum protocols when perfect deterministic execution isn't required. The mathematical framework also reveals deep connections between quantum channel structure and simulation complexity, opening new avenues for understanding what makes certain quantum operations fundamentally harder to implement than others.

We study the problem of implementing a quantum channel using a small ancilla with classical randomization and postselection on an output failure flag. The simulation is probabilistic, but exact conditioned on success. We prove that any unital channel on a $d$-dimensional system can be simulated with ancilla dimension $k$ and success probability $Ω(\frac{k}{\log d})$. Equivalently, every unital channel on $n$ qubits can be simulated with constant success probability using only $O(\log n)$ ancillary qubits. We show that this tradeoff is best possible by constructing a family of channels which cannot be simulated with success probability better than $O(\frac{k}{\log d})$. We also show that the class of \textit{highly noncommutative} channels, which includes random channels, admits constant-success simulation with just a single ancillary qubit. We further show that this model of simulation necessarily fails for strongly non-unital channels and discuss possible extensions involving adaptivity. On the technical side we rely on a partition-based protocol and matrix concentration inequalities, including the recent refinement of noncommutative Khintchine inequalities due to Bandeira, Boedihardjo and van Handel.

The ability to run nine different quantum error-correcting codes on a single 40-qubit trapped-ion processor without hardware reconfiguration showcases remarkable versatility and suggests these systems are approaching the threshold where error correction becomes beneficial rather than burdensome. Detailed in a technical manuscript deposited on the open-access arXiv repository, the research team utilized a linear chain of forty [...] The post IonQ Experimental Demonstration of Breakeven qLDPC and Block Codes on a Trapped-Ion Architecture appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scientists identify the origin of noise in spin qubit quantum processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a crucial step toward practical quantum computers by pinpointing the sources of decoherence that plague spin qubit systems, which are among the most promising candidates for large-scale quantum processors due to their compatibility with existing semiconductor fabrication. Understanding and controlling noise is absolutely essential for quantum error correction to work effectively, as even the most sophisticated error correction codes can only handle errors below certain thresholds. The fact that spin qubits are now achieving fidelities that exceed requirements for surface code implementation is particularly exciting, as this puts them in serious contention with other leading qubit technologies like superconducting circuits. By identifying specific noise mechanisms, researchers can now engineer targeted solutions to push spin qubit performance even further, potentially accelerating the timeline for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

A spin qubit, in which quantum information is encoded in the spin state of an electron, is one of the most promising platforms for quantum computing. Spin qubits exhibit long coherence times and are compatible with advanced semiconductor manufacturing technologies. The leading implementation of spin qubits involves confined electrons inside quantum dots, a nanoscale semiconductor architecture that behaves like a controllable artificial atom. Recent advances have enabled high-fidelity operation of single- and two-qubit gates, exceeding the threshold required for certain surface code quantum error correction techniques.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Ferroelectrical Switching as a Probe of Quantum Damping in Magnetic Spin Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental challenge in quantum magnetism by proposing an elegant experimental method to detect quantum effects in magnetic damping, which have remained frustratingly difficult to observe despite decades of theoretical predictions. The team's approach using ferroelectric substrates to electrically switch magnetic interactions between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic states offers a practical pathway to control and measure quantum entanglement in magnetic systems. What makes this particularly exciting is the connection between readily observable magnetization dynamics and the underlying quantum entanglement, potentially opening the door to voltage-controlled quantum devices based on magnetic materials. This work bridges the gap between fundamental quantum many-body physics and practical quantum technologies, offering a new platform for exploring quantum effects in condensed matter systems.

While damped spin dynamics is important for the understanding of magnetic materials, clear signatures of \emph{quantum corrections} to the Gilbert damping mechanism remain elusive. We propose a route to distinguish quantum and classical Gilbert spin damping using ferroelectric control of a magnetic dimer. Ab initio calculations for dimers on ferroelectric substrates show that polarization reversal switches the inter-spin exchange between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic regimes. We formulate a magnetization-based diagnostic that relates magnetization traces to entanglement dynamics, which enables ferroelectrical on/off control of dimer entanglement. Material-informed quantum Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert simulations illustrate how the signature of magnetization dynamics can, in principle, be used to infer the existence of quantum Gilbert spin damping. This minimal and non-volatile platform connects first-principles modeling to experimentally accessible observables and provides a starting point for voltage-controlled quantum entanglement in magnetic spin networks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Coherent versus stochastic error injection on a repetition-code logical qubit in superconducting hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a crucial real-world challenge in quantum error correction: the gap between theoretical predictions and experimental reality when dealing with different types of noise. While theory suggests that coherent errors (which maintain quantum phase relationships) should behave very differently from random stochastic errors in repetition codes, the IBM team found no such difference in their superconducting quantum processor experiments. The suspected culprit is frequency drift in the qubits themselves, which appears to scramble coherent errors into effectively random ones, highlighting how subtle hardware imperfections can dramatically alter error correction performance. This work underscores the importance of bridging theory and practice in quantum error correction, showing that even small, seemingly innocuous effects like qubit drift can reshape how errors propagate through quantum circuits.

The performance of quantum error correction (QEC) codes is limited by the underlying physical noise. Theoretical studies show that coherent and stochastic noise have different effects when performing QEC with either surface or repetition codes. We use the bitflip repetition code, realized in a transmon quantum processor, as a testbed to experimentally study the impact of injecting coherent versus stochastic errors on the logical performance. We adapt a scalable free-fermion simulator to simulate the experiments and we modify a subset sampling technique to efficiently sample stochastic noise in the quantum circuit. In the experiment, we do not observe the difference in logical fidelity predicted by simulation for either the distance-3 or distance-5 repetition codes. We hypothesize that this discrepancy could be explained by small drifts in qubit frequencies, which introduce phase-coherent noise that `stochastifies' the injected coherent errors. Our work contributes to advancing an understanding of how coherent errors affect experimental QEC.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Suppression of Quasiparticle Poisoning to $10^{-11}$ Levels in Superconducting Qubits via Infrared Shielding
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough tackles one of the most persistent challenges in superconducting quantum computing: quasiparticle poisoning, where unwanted energy excitations corrupt qubit states and destroy quantum information. By implementing sophisticated infrared shielding, the researchers achieved a staggering four-order-of-magnitude reduction in quasiparticle density to just 1.88×10^-11 per Cooper pair - the lowest ever reported - essentially creating an ultra-clean quantum environment where qubits can maintain coherence far longer. The work demonstrates that with proper engineering of the electromagnetic environment around quantum processors, initialization errors can be reduced to just 0.01%, a critical milestone for building the large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers that major tech companies are racing to develop. This represents a significant step toward solving the scalability problem that has long plagued superconducting quantum systems, showing that environmental control may be just as important as the qubits themselves.

Quasiparticle poisoning bottlenecks superconducting qubits, limiting coherence and the scalability of quantum processors. In this work, we systematically investigate quasiparticle poisoning in superconducting qubits under three infrared (IR) shielding configurations, ranging from a dedicated multi-layer design to a simplified implementation. By measuring quasiparticle-induced parity switching, we demonstrate a suppression of the switching rate by over four orders of magnitude via the implementation of improved shielding. In the best configuration, the rate decreases over time following cooldown and reaches 0.069$\,$Hz on day 34, corresponding to an anticipated quasiparticle density per Cooper pair of $1.88\times10^{-11}$. To our knowledge, this represents the lowest quasiparticle density reported in the literature to date. The remaining quasiparticle population is likely dominated by sporadic phonon bursts stemming from mechanical stress release in the on-chip films, as well as from the surrounding environment. The effective qubit temperature follows the phonon bath down to 17$\,$mK, enabling initialization errors of $\sim 0.01\%$ for 3$\,$GHz qubits. These results demonstrate that proper IR shielding and thermalization are essential for suppressing quasiparticle poisoning and enabling high-coherence, scalable superconducting qubit systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics

item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Experimental Demonstration of Free-Space Unidimensional Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution Under High Detector Noise
item.description:
Curator's Take: This experimental work tackles one of the most stubborn practical challenges in quantum cryptography: making continuous-variable quantum key distribution work reliably in free-space environments where detector noise typically kills the quantum advantage. The researchers achieved a respectable 270 kbps secure key rate using a simplified unidimensional approach that requires only standard telecom equipment, making quantum-secured communications more accessible than complex discrete-variable systems. However, the stark difference between trusted and untrusted detector noise models reveals a critical vulnerability - the system only works when you can guarantee your detectors haven't been tampered with, which significantly limits real-world deployment scenarios. This study provides valuable insights for the quantum communication community about the fundamental trade-offs between system simplicity, noise tolerance, and security assumptions in practical quantum cryptography implementations.

Continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD), which uses quadratures of the electromagnetic field, enables practical quantum communication using standard telecommunication technologies. Unidimensional CV-QKD (UD-CVQKD) simplifies the implementation by restricting modulation to a single quadrature. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate a free-space Gaussian-modulated UD-CVQKD system operating under a high detector electronic-noise regime (1.4 shot-noise units). The system employs polarized coherent states with signal and local oscillator co-propagating in the same spatial mode in orthogonal polarizations, ensuring stable interference. System security is analyzed under both untrusted (UTD) and trusted (TD) detector noise models. While no positive secret key rate is obtained under the UTD model, the TD model enables secure key generation over a finite range of modulation variances, highlighting the critical role of detector trust in high-noise conditions. A maximum secret key rate of 270 kbps is achieved at an optimal modulation variance of 11.57. Furthermore, secure operation requires high-transmittance (low-loss) channels under such noise conditions. This study demonstrates the practical feasibility of free-space UD-CVQKD in realistic high electronic-noise detection constraints and highlights detector electronic noise as a key limiting factor in practical systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics

item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Projector Quantum Variational Ansatz
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research bridges a crucial gap between near-term NISQ algorithms and future fault-tolerant quantum computing by developing a variational ansatz that mimics the projector-based approach used in powerful FTQC algorithms like Quantum Phase Estimation. The work is particularly significant because it offers a potential pathway to access some benefits of fault-tolerant algorithms within current noisy quantum hardware limitations, while maintaining the shallow circuit depths that ADAPT-VQE provides. By creating a hybrid approach that can switch between ISQ-QSP and ADAPT-VQE structures depending on parametrization, this could help quantum computing researchers better prepare for the eventual transition from NISQ to fault-tolerant systems. This represents an important step toward making quantum algorithms more robust and scalable across different hardware generations.

Quantum computing offers several algorithms to compute the ground state of a problem Hamiltonian. The most desirable algorithms belong to the Fault Tolerant QuantumComputing (FTQC) regime, such as quantum algorithms with repetitive structure like Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) and Quantum Signal Processing (QSP). However, in the Noisy In-termediate Scale Quantum (NISQ) regime, the most realistic approaches involve Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) algorithms and their variants. VQE is an algorithm that searches for a parametrized unitary matrix called an ansatz whose purposeis to transform an easily prepared initial state into the groundstate of a given Hamiltonian. Adaptive Derivative-AssembledPseudo-Trotter (ADAPT)-VQE is a variant of VQE that im-proves this approach by constructing the ansatz iteratively so that the associated quantum circuit is as shallow as possible. A major difference between FTQC (i.e. not variational) algorithms and VQE is that FTQC algorithms do not construct a state transitiondirectly. Instead, they construct a projector that identifies the ground state using ancillary qubits that flag the good solution. The desired state is then obtained via amplitude amplification orpost-selection. In this work, we propose a VQE ansatz whose structure is more similar to that of an FTQC algorithm. Depending on its parametrization, this ansatz can be equivalent to either an Intermediate Scale Quantum (ISQ)-QSP or to an ADAPT-VQE quantum circuit structure. Our experimental results show that this first proposal of Projector Variational Ansatz (PVA) converges with a shallower ansatz than the usual ADAPT-VQE.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum correlations and coherence in a two-qubit anisotropic $XY$ under magnetic field
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical study provides crucial insights for quantum hardware designers by mapping out exactly how different types of quantum correlations degrade under realistic conditions like temperature fluctuations and magnetic field variations. The research reveals a fascinating hierarchy where quantum coherence proves most resilient to thermal noise, outlasting even entanglement - a finding that could guide the development of more robust quantum processors that leverage coherence-based protocols. Particularly valuable is the identification of how magnetic anisotropy can prevent the abrupt "sudden death" of entanglement, instead allowing for smoother degradation that might be easier to manage in practical quantum systems. These results offer a theoretical roadmap for optimizing qubit designs and operating conditions to maximize quantum advantage in noisy, real-world environments.

We study thermal quantum correlations and coherence in Heisenberg $XY$ model with anisotropic interactions under a uniform magnetic field $ B $. Using concurrence $C$, local quantum uncertainty (LQU), Bell-Clauser-Horne-Shimony-Holt (CHSH) nonlocality $ \mathbb{B}$, and coherence $C_l$ as quantifiers, we analyze how magnetic anisotropy $ δ_m $, coupling anisotropy $ δ_c $, Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction $ D $, temperature $ T $, and magnetic field $ B $ modulate quantum resources. At low temperatures and relevant magnetic fields, the entanglement is maximized, but exhibits sudden death for $ δ_m = 0 $, which turns into a smooth decay as $ δ_m $ increases, highlighting its stabilizing role. LQU shows that stronger anisotropy suppresses quantum correlations, while $ \mathbb{B} $ induces a non-monotonic response peaking at a critical field $ B_c $. Bell-CHSH nonlocality violations ($ \mathbb{B} > 2 $) persist below $ B_c $, but thermal noise ($ T \geq 1 $) suppresses them. Coherence $ C_l $ is most robust to thermal fluctuations, especially for high \( δ_m \), which also dampens abrupt quantum phase transitions. The DM interaction is essential for entanglement generation, with $ D $ and anisotropy synergistically enhancing correlation resilience. We identify a hierarchy of thermal degradation: nonlocality ($ \mathbb{B} $) vanishes first, followed by entanglement ($ C $), then general quantum correlations (LQU), while coherence $ C_l $ persists the longest. These results demonstrate tunable control of quantum resources via anisotropy and external parameters, providing insights for the design of robust spin-based quantum technologies.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Exact noise characterization of entanglement distribution in star networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing practical challenges in quantum networking: understanding exactly how noise accumulates when distributing multipartite entangled states across star-shaped networks, which are expected to be the first scalable quantum network architectures. The work provides crucial analytical tools that allow network designers to optimize protocols without expensive simulations, addressing the fundamental problem that quantum memories decohere while waiting for all network links to be established successfully. By comparing factory and piecemaker distribution protocols and deriving exact noise characterizations, this study offers essential insights for building real-world quantum networks where timing uncertainties and memory limitations are unavoidable constraints. The mathematical framework developed here could significantly accelerate the development of practical quantum communication networks by enabling precise performance predictions and protocol optimization.

Multipartite entanglement forms the core of many networking applications. In the near-term future, it is expected that multipartite distribution will be achieved first through star topologies, making it important to understand the noise incurred during the distribution process. In such networks, elementary links are created stochastically and successful links must be stored while waiting for the remaining links, causing memory decoherence that depends on the random waiting times. We derive analytical expressions for both the average noise and its distribution, when distributing GHZ states under memory dephasing in star networks. We study and compare two distribution protocols: the factory and piecemaker protocol. Furthermore, we find expressions for the case of a global cut-off (allowing fast optimization of the cut-off without requiring Monte Carlo simulations) and extend the analysis for the factory protocol to depolarizing noise for arbitrary states.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Solution of the Equation-of-Motion Phonon Method eigenvalue problems on the D-Wave quantum annealer
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research demonstrates a clever workaround for one of quantum computing's most promising but currently impractical applications - solving the massive eigenvalue problems that arise in nuclear physics simulations. While the theoretically optimal Quantum Phase Estimation algorithm remains out of reach for today's noisy quantum hardware, these researchers successfully adapted D-Wave's quantum annealer to tackle real nuclear many-body calculations by reformulating eigenvalue problems as optimization tasks. The hybrid approach, which combines quantum annealing with classical deflation techniques to extract complete eigenspectra, represents exactly the kind of near-term quantum advantage we need to see - taking quantum hardware as it exists today and finding meaningful scientific applications. This work could open doors for quantum-assisted materials science and nuclear engineering calculations well before fault-tolerant quantum computers become available.

The solution of large-scale eigenvalue problems is crucial in nuclear many-body theory, where Hamiltonian matrices often reach extremely large dimensions. Quantum computing opens new perspectives for addressing such demanding problems. Although the Quantum Phase Estimation algorithm offers, in principle, a systematic route to matrix diagonalization, its practical deployment demands levels of coherence and error correction that current quantum hardware cannot yet support. A viable near-term strategy is instead to exploit quantum annealing, which enables the recasting of eigenvalue problems into quadratic unconstrained binary optimization formulations that can be addressed by existing annealing-based processors. Here, we propose a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm that combines quantum annealing and classical deflation to iteratively extract the full eigenspectrum of both standard and generalized eigenvalue problems. We benchmark this method on eigenvalue problems arising from the Equation of Motion Phonon Method performing calculations on real quantum hardware. Our approach illustrates the capabilities and limitations of near-term quantum devices in addressing nuclear eigenvalue problems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: OQC, JPMorganChase and AMD Commence Research Collaboration to Develop New Quantum-AI Platform in London
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration represents a significant step toward practical quantum-AI integration, with JPMorganChase becoming one of the first major financial institutions to test quantum algorithms in a dedicated enterprise-grade environment rather than relying solely on cloud access. The partnership combines OQC's quantum hardware expertise with AMD's classical computing infrastructure, creating a hybrid platform that could accelerate the development of quantum advantage applications in financial modeling and risk analysis. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the focus on building secure, on-premises quantum infrastructure in London, potentially establishing a template for how other enterprises might deploy quantum computing capabilities within their own data centers rather than depending entirely on external quantum cloud services.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — OQC, JPMorganChase and AMD today announced a research collaboration leveraging a new and dedicated Quantum-AI Data Centre, built by OQC in London. JPMorganChase researchers will test near-term quantum and hybrid quantum-classical computing applications via a secure enterprise environment to examine how quantum computing, AI and high-performance classical infrastructure can work […]
