item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in an asymmetric Fabry-Perot cavity
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research breaks new ground by demonstrating that asymmetric Fabry-Perot cavities can generate nonreciprocal quantum entanglement, where quantum correlations behave differently depending on direction - a quantum analog of how certain materials allow light to pass through more easily in one direction than the other. What makes this particularly intriguing is the surprising finding that classical nonreciprocity (directional transmission) and quantum nonreciprocity (directional entanglement) don't necessarily correlate as one might expect, suggesting these phenomena operate through fundamentally different mechanisms. The enhanced robustness of entanglement in asymmetric cavities compared to symmetric ones could prove valuable for quantum sensing and communication applications, where maintaining quantum correlations in noisy environments remains a major challenge. This work opens up a new research direction at the intersection of quantum optics and mechanical systems, potentially leading to novel quantum devices that exploit directional quantum effects.

Nonreciprocal transmission (classical nonreciprocity) in optomechanical systems based on asymmetric Fabry-Perot (F-P) cavities has been theoretically proposed and experimentally demonstrated. However, nonreciprocal quantum effects, particularly nonreciprocal quantum entanglement, remain unexplored in such systems. Here, we propose to generate nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in an asymmetric F-P cavity and discuss the connection between the nonreciprocal transmission and nonreciprocal quantum entanglement. We reproduce the nonreciprocal transmission spectra by solving the quantum Langevin equations, and then discuss the optimal parameters to achieve nonreciprocal optomechanical entanglement in the system. We show that a greater and more robust optomechanical entanglement can be approached in the asymmetric F-P cavities, in comparing with the symmetric cavities. Furthermore, we find that the degrees of classical and quantum nonreciprocities do not exhibit positive correlation as expected. Our work shows that the classical and quantum nonreciprocities can be realized simultaneously in the asymmetric F-P cavities, which provide a platform to explore the connection between classical and quantum nonreciprocities.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable Quantum Algorithms for Gutzwiller Projection
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical bottleneck in quantum simulation by developing a dramatically more efficient way to prepare Gutzwiller-projected BCS states, which are essential starting points for studying strongly correlated quantum materials like high-temperature superconductors. The amplitude amplification technique delivers a quadratic speedup over traditional measurement-based approaches, translating to a staggering seven-orders-of-magnitude reduction in computational overhead for realistic 100-site systems. While the projected-state weights still decrease exponentially with system size, this algorithmic breakthrough makes previously intractable quantum simulations of complex materials feasible on near-term fault-tolerant quantum computers. The work represents a significant step toward using quantum computers to solve some of condensed matter physics' most challenging problems, particularly in understanding exotic phases of matter where classical methods fall short.

Quantum simulation requires highly accurate input states. Gutzwiller-projected Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) states provide physically motivated input states for solving strongly correlated lattice models, but their preparation on a quantum computer is hindered by the non-trivial nature of the Gutzwiller projection. We construct scalable quantum algorithms for this task by combining a circuit construction for arbitrary BCS states with the amplitude amplification for Gutzwiller projection (AAGP) procedure. AAGP yields a quadratic reduction in the number of projection queries compared with measurement-based postselection and leads to substantially improved fault-tolerant resource scaling. For projected BCS states optimized for the square-lattice $t$-$J$ model, we find that the projected-state weight decreases exponentially with system size, but the quadratic improvement is still large enough at physically relevant finite sizes to make a decisive practical difference. In particular, for a 100-site benchmark, AAGP reduces the required number of projection queries by about seven orders of magnitude. These results establish AAGP as an enabling input-state preparation protocol for projected BCS states in quantum simulation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: C12 Automates Pick & Place Nanoassembly to Standardize Carbon Nanotube Qubit Fabrication
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development marks a crucial step toward making carbon nanotube qubits manufacturable at scale, addressing one of the biggest challenges facing this promising quantum computing platform. C12's Pick & Place process cleverly separates the high-temperature nanotube growth from the delicate quantum circuit fabrication, potentially solving the thermal incompatibility issues that have plagued CNT qubit production. Carbon nanotubes offer unique advantages like naturally long coherence times and single-atom precision, but until now have been notoriously difficult to integrate into reliable manufacturing workflows. This automation breakthrough could position carbon nanotube qubits as serious competitors to superconducting and trapped-ion systems by making them economically viable for quantum processors.

Quantum hardware venture C12 has introduced Pick & Place, a patented nanoassembly process engineered to transfer single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) onto pre-fabricated quantum circuits with micrometric precision. The fabrication method serves as a foundational manufacturing block to decouple high-temperature nanotube growth from sensitive, sub-micron chip lithography layers. item.title: Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Sign Agreement to Integrate Trapped-Ion Hardware into Industrial Design Lifecycles
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant step toward quantum computing's integration into real-world industrial applications, moving beyond academic research toward practical engineering workflows. By combining Quantinuum's high-fidelity trapped-ion systems with Mitsubishi Electric's established industrial simulation expertise, the collaboration could accelerate quantum advantage in complex manufacturing optimization problems where classical computers struggle with exponential scaling. The focus on integrating quantum hardware directly into existing design lifecycles is particularly noteworthy, as it addresses the critical challenge of making quantum computing accessible to engineers who aren't quantum specialists. While still early-stage with the non-binding MOU, this type of industry-quantum hardware partnership could serve as a template for bringing quantum benefits to traditional engineering sectors.

Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Corporation have signed a non-binding Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to build a joint development framework for advanced industrial engineering and manufacturing design. The partnership aims to combine Quantinuum’s high-fidelity trapped-ion hardware platforms with Mitsubishi Electric’s extensive industrial simulation libraries. item.title: Quantinuum Prices Upsized Initial Public Offering at $1.68 Billion to Landmark Public Market Debut
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a watershed moment for the quantum computing industry as Quantinuum becomes one of the first major quantum hardware companies to go public, raising $1.68 billion in what represents the largest quantum computing IPO to date. The successful upsizing of their offering signals strong investor confidence in trapped-ion quantum computing technology and validates the commercial potential of quantum systems beyond just research applications. Quantinuum's public debut provides crucial capital for scaling their quantum hardware and software platforms while establishing a benchmark valuation that will likely influence funding decisions across the entire quantum ecosystem. This IPO represents a maturation milestone for quantum computing, transitioning from a purely venture-backed sector to attracting mainstream public market investment.

Quantinuum Inc. has finalized the pricing of its upsized initial public offering (IPO) on the Nasdaq Global Market under the ticker symbol QNT. The transaction structure issued 28,000,000 shares of Class A common stock at a public offering price of $60.00 per share, expanding the initial capital targets to accumulate approximately $1.68 billion in gross proceeds.
